title: Numerical study of the shape parameter dependence of the local radial point interpolation method in linear elasticity
author: Moussaoui, Bouziane

steps.title: Method detail
steps.instruction: Meshless method has attracted more and more attention from researchers in recent years.It is regarded as a potential numerical method in computational mechanics. Several meshless methods, such as smooth particle hydrodynamics (SPH) method [1], [2], element free Galerkin (EFG) method [3], meshless local Petrov-Galerkin (MLPG) method [4], [5], [6], [7], [8], point interpolation method (PIM) [9], [10], local point interpolation method (LPIM) [11] and local radial point interpolation method (LRPIM) has been proposed by Liu et al. [10], [12], [13]. In LRPIM, the point interpolation using the radial basis function to construct the shape functions which have the delta function property. The radial basis function (RBFs) is thin plate spline (TPS) [14], [15]. Local weak forms are developed using weighted residual method locally from the partial differential equation of linear elasticity of 2D solids. The number of numerical examples will be presented to demonstrate the convergence and accuracy, validity and efficiency of the present methods. The local radial point interpolation method LRPIM is a Meshless method with properties of simple implementation of the essential boundary conditions and with the lower cost than moving least squares (MLS) methods.

steps.title: Method detail
steps.instruction: This paper deals with the effect of sizing parameter of subdomains on the convergence and accuracy of the methods. Numerical values will be presented to specify the convergence domain by precising maximum and minimum values as a function of distribution nodes number and by using the radial basis functions TPS (thin plate spline). It also will present a comparison with numerical results for different materials. The analytical solution of the deflection confirms the numerical results. The LRPIM method will be developed to solve the problem of a thin elastic homogenous plate. The local weak form and numerical implementation are presented in section 3, numerical example for 2D problem are given in section 4. Then, the paper will end with the results, discussions and finally the conclusions.

steps.title: Local weak form method LRPIM
steps.instruction: Let us consider a two-dimensional problem of solid mechanics in domain Ω bounded by Г whose strong-form of governing equation and the essential boundary conditions are given by:(7)σij,j(x)+bi(x)=0(8)σijnj=ti0onΓt(9)ui=ui0onΓuwhere in Ω: σT=[σxx,σyy,τxy] is the stress vector, bT=[bx,by] the body force vector. n=(n1,n2) is the vector of unit outward normal at a point on the natural boundaries.

steps.title: Local weak form method LRPIM
steps.instruction: t0is the prescribed effort, [u1,u2] are the displacement components in the plan and [u10,u20] are the prescribed displacement on the essential boundaries.

steps.title: Local weak form method LRPIM
steps.instruction: In the local Petrov-Galerkin approache [3], one may use a weak form over ΩQ a local quadrature domain (for node I), which may have an arbitrary shape, and contain the point xQ in question, see Fig. 1. The generalized local weak form of the differential Eq. (7) is obtained by:(10)∫ΩQ(σij,j(x)+b(x)i)υIdΩ=0where ΩQ is the local domain of quadrature for node I and υI is the weight or test function (υI∈CK(Ω)) [4].

steps.title: Local weak form method LRPIM
steps.instruction: Generally, in meshfree methods, the representation of field nodes in the domain will be associated to other repartitions of problem domain: ΩI is the influence domain for nodes interpolation, ΩS is the support domain for accuracy. For each node Ωυ is the weight function domain and ΩQ is the quadrature domain for local integration.

steps.title: Local weak form method LRPIM
steps.instruction: Using the divergence theorem [4] in Eq. (10), we obtain:(11)∫ΓQσijnjυIdΓ−∫ΩQσijυI,jdΩ+∫ΩQbiυIdΩ=0where ΓQ=ΓQi∪ΓQu∪ΓQt

steps.title: Local weak form method LRPIM
steps.instruction: ΓQi: The internal boundary of the quadrature domain

steps.title: Local weak form method LRPIM
steps.instruction: ΓQt: The part of the natural boundary that intersects with the quadrature domain

steps.title: Local weak form method LRPIM
steps.instruction: ΓQu: The part of the essential boundary that intersects with the quadrature domain

steps.title: Local weak form method LRPIM
steps.instruction: We can then change the expression of Eq. (11):(12)∫ΓQiσijnjυIdΓ+∫ΓQuσijnjυIdΓ+∫ΓQtσijnjυIdΓ−∫ΩQσijυI,jdΩ+∫ΩQbiυIdΩ=0

steps.instruction: Using the radial point interpolation method (RPIM) shape functions (see sub-section 2), we can approximate the trial function for the displacement at a point x (∀x∈ΩS) as Eq. (6)

steps.instruction: Substituting Eqs. (15) and (16) into Eq. (14), we obtain the discret systems of linear equations for the node I.

steps.instruction: The matrix form of Eq. (17) can be written as in matrix form:(18)∑I=1ntKIuI=fIwhere KI is a 2 × 2 matrix called a nodal stiffness matrix, given by(19)K|=∫ΩQV¯ITCB|dΩ−∫ΓQtLnTCB|VIdΓ−∫ΓQuLnTCB|VIdΓ

steps.title: Local weak form method LRPIM
steps.instruction: and nodal force vector with contributions from body forces applied in the problem domains:(20)fI=∫ΓQit0VIdΓ+∫ΩQVIbdΩwhere n0 denote the set of the nodes in the support domain ΩS of point xQ.

steps.title: Local weak form method LRPIM
steps.instruction: Two independent linear equations can be obtained for each node in the entire problem domain and by assembling all these n × 2 equations to obtain the final global system equations:(21)k2n×2t⁡u2n⁡×1=f2n×1

steps.title: Local weak form method LRPIM
steps.instruction: To solve the precedent system, the standard Gauss quadrature formula is applied with 16 G points [3], [22] for calculating integrals in Eqs. (19) and (20) on both boundary and domain.

steps.title: Local weak form method LRPIM
steps.instruction: The size of quadrature domain is specified by setting αQ=2 and a regular distribution of nodes on the mid-surface of plate in (x, y) plane is employed.
